skill.name: abstract-trimmer
skill.description: Precision editing tool that reduces abstract word count through intelligent compression techniques, maintaining scientific rigor while meeting strict journal and conference requirements.
skill.summary: Reduces academic abstract word count using intelligent compression while preserving scientific accuracy and key findings.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 59 patterns across SKILL.md, scripts/main.py, and the audit result file. After manual evaluation, 57 of 59 findings are false positives: backtick detections are markdown code fences, cryptographic algorithm detections have no basis in the code, and URL, reconnaissance, and path-traversal detections are documentation artifacts or security checklist items referencing what the skill does not do. The only confirmed findings are expected file read and write operations in scripts/main.py at lines 141-144 and 178-179, which are legitimate functionality for a text processing tool that reads abstracts and writes trimmed output.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled File Write Path
low_findings.description: The script writes output to a user-specified file path via the --output argument without path validation or sandboxing. While this is expected behavior for a text processing tool, it could allow writing to arbitrary locations if the execution environment is not properly sandboxed.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled File Read Path
low_findings.description: The script reads from a user-specified file path via the --input argument. A malicious prompt could direct the agent to read sensitive files outside the intended workspace.

low_findings.title: Command Execution with User-Supplied Text
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the AI agent to execute Python commands with user-supplied abstract text passed via --text argument. While argparse handles argument parsing safely and only the skill's own script is executed, user-provided content flows into a shell command.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Write to User-Controlled Path
dangerous_patterns.description: open(args.output, 'w') writes to any path specified by the user. No path validation, sandboxing, or workspace restriction is applied. This is standard CLI behavior but presents risk in an AI agent execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Read from User-Controlled Path
dangerous_patterns.description: open(args.input, 'r') reads from any path specified by the user. An attacker could craft a prompt directing the agent to read sensitive files.

content.user_title: Trim Academic Abstracts to Meet Word Limits
content.value_statement: Academic abstracts often exceed journal word limits, forcing rushed last-minute revisions. This skill uses intelligent compression to reduce word count while preserving scientific accuracy, key statistics, and structural integrity.
actual_capabilities: Reduce abstract word count using conservative, balanced, or aggressive compression strategies
Preserve quantitative data including P-values, confidence intervals, and numerical results during trimming
Maintain the Background-Methods-Results-Conclusion structure throughout the compression process
Process abstracts from files, command-line text input, or standard input
Output trimmed results in JSON format with statistics or plain text
Report word count statistics including original count, final count, and reduction percentage
limitations: Optimized for English academic abstracts only; other languages may produce inconsistent results
Does not rewrite or rephrase content; only removes redundant words and common phrases
Automated trimming requires human review to verify scientific accuracy and readability
Expects structured abstract format; unstructured text may lose coherence during aggressive trimming

use_cases.title: Journal Submission Preparation
use_cases.description: A researcher needs to trim a 350-word abstract to fit a journal's 250-word limit before the submission deadline, preserving all key findings and statistics.

use_cases.title: Conference Abstract Compliance
use_cases.description: A graduate student must reduce multiple abstracts to meet a conference's strict word count requirements while maintaining the core message of each study.

use_cases.title: Grant Proposal Abstract Optimization
use_cases.description: A principal investigator needs to compress a detailed project summary into a concise abstract that fits the funding agency's character limit without losing essential details.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Word Count Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the word count of my abstract and tell me if it meets a 250-word limit.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to check if their abstract is within limits before making any edits

prompt_templates.title: Trim Abstract to Target Word Count
prompt_templates.prompt: Trim my abstract in abstract.txt to 200 words using the balanced strategy. Show me the before and after word counts.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has an abstract file and needs to meet a specific journal word limit

prompt_templates.title: Aggressive Trimming with Output File
prompt_templates.prompt: Aggressively trim my abstract to 150 words, save the result to trimmed_abstract.txt, and report the reduction percentage. The abstract is in draft.txt.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs maximum compression and wants the result saved to a separate file

prompt_templates.title: Batch Check Multiple Abstracts
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the word counts of every .txt file in my abstracts folder against a 300-word target. List which files exceed the limit and by how many words.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has multiple abstracts to evaluate against a common word limit before a conference deadline

output_examples.input: Trim this abstract to 40 words: 'Background: Hypertension affects millions worldwide. Methods: We conducted a randomized trial of 500 patients comparing lifestyle coaching to standard care over 12 weeks. Results: Blood pressure improved significantly in the coaching group (P<0.01). Conclusion: Lifestyle coaching is an effective intervention for hypertension management.'
output_examples.output: Trimmed abstract (28 words): Hypertension affects millions. In a randomized trial of 500 patients, lifestyle coaching significantly improved blood pressure compared to standard care (P<0.01).

output_examples.input: Check the word count of my abstract: 'This study examined the effects of sleep deprivation on cognitive performance in college students using a controlled experiment design.'
output_examples.output: Current word count: 21. Target word count: 250. Within limit: 229 words under target.

best_practices: Always review trimmed abstracts manually to verify that key scientific findings remain accurate and complete
Start with the conservative strategy and increase to balanced or aggressive only if more word reduction is needed
Use check-only mode first to understand how far over the limit your abstract is before applying any trimming
anti_patterns: Do not use aggressive trimming on abstracts with critical numerical data without verifying results manually afterward
Do not submit a trimmed abstract for publication without human review; the tool removes text patterns, not based on semantic importance
Do not expect the tool to rewrite poorly written abstracts; it only compresses existing text and cannot improve clarity

faq.question: What types of abstracts does this skill support?
faq.answer: It works best with structured scientific abstracts following the Background, Methods, Results, Conclusion format written in English.

faq.question: Can the skill rewrite or improve my abstract?
faq.answer: No, it only removes redundant words and compresses text using pattern matching. It does not rephrase, rewrite, or improve writing quality.

faq.question: What is the difference between conservative, balanced, and aggressive strategies?
faq.answer: Conservative removes only filler words for minor reductions. Balanced also condenses common academic phrases. Aggressive removes transition words and secondary details for maximum word reduction.

faq.question: Will the skill preserve my statistical data like P-values?
faq.answer: The skill uses pattern-based compression and does not specifically protect numbers. Always verify that P-values, confidence intervals, and statistics remain intact after trimming.

faq.question: Is the trimmed output ready for journal submission?
faq.answer: No, automated trimming should be treated as a first pass. Human review is required to ensure scientific accuracy, grammatical correctness, and readability.

faq.question: Can I process multiple abstracts at once?
faq.answer: The skill processes one abstract per command. For batch processing, you can run the tool in a shell loop over multiple files in a directory.
